MSL-Bench
– problems · 4 tiers –
01 .kernelbench()

LLM-generated Metal kernels, timed on GPU.

Can a language model write GPU code that actually runs — and beats the hand-tuned libraries GPUs ship with? Every kernel below is a real .metal file, compiled with Apple's own xcrun metal, executed on Apple Silicon, verified against Metal Performance Shaders (MPS, Apple's own GPU compute library), and timed with GPU timestamps. Nothing simulated.

The headline metric is fast_p: the fraction of problems where the model's kernel was both correct AND ran at least p× as fast as MPS. So fast_1 = 40% means the model got 40% of problems right with kernels that matched or beat Apple's implementation.

02 .visualize()

Three angles on the same data. The fast_p curve is the money chart — it shows cumulative correctness across every speedup threshold. A curve staying high past the = MPS dashed line means the model isn't just writing correct kernels; it's writing ones that match or beat Apple's own implementation.

The tier bars break correctness down by difficulty. T1 is elementwise math like out[i] = f(x[i]). T2 is reductions (row sums, softmax) — the first tier requiring cross-thread coordination via threadgroup memory and barriers. T3 is tiled kernels (matmul, convolution). T4 is fused ops (attention, layernorm, GLU) — the actual building blocks of modern LLMs. The T1 → T2 cliff is universal across every model tested: everyone handles elementwise Metal, everyone cliff-dives on cross-thread coordination.

Failure modes shows where each run breaks — green is correct, grays are the ways things went wrong. A shorter green bar with lots of gray = the model reached for something ambitious but couldn't land it.

fast_p curves

Fraction of problems correct AND reaching ≥ p × the PyTorch MPS reference speed. Higher curve = better model. Dashed line at p=1 = matches MPS.

Correctness by tier

fast_0 by difficulty tier (T1 elementwise → T4 attention). Empty slot = no problems attempted at that tier.

Where each run fails

Attempted problems by outcome. Green = correct; grays = failure modes.
03 .leaderboard()

Ranked runs · sorted by fast_1

Sorted by fast_1 — the fraction of problems where the model's kernel matched or beat MPS speed. Each row is one model on one attempt strategy: one_shot means the model gets one try per problem, no retries. repair k=5 means the model gets to see its compile errors and try again — up to 5 attempts per problem — with each failed attempt's error text fed back into the next prompt.

Rows marked † are partial samples where a free-tier quota wall cut the sweep short. Numbers from partial runs aren't directly comparable to full-suite runs. n = number of problems attempted (out of 60).

Run n fast_0 · correct fast_1 · ≥ MPS fast_2 · ≥ 2×
04 .grid()

Per-problem outcome · click any cell for the kernel source

Every cell is one model's attempt on one problem. Click any cell to see the actual Metal kernel the model produced. Rows are grouped by tier (T1 → T4) then problem id; columns are the runs from the leaderboard.

✓ 1.1× compiled, ran, matched MPS reference (speedup shown)
✓ 2.5× correct AND ≥2× MPS speed
v compiled + ran but produced wrong numbers
c invalid MSL syntax — didn't compile
r crashed at execution
e API / harness hiccup (not the model's fault)
· not attempted for this run
TIER · T1 elementwise (out[i] = f(x[i])) · T2 reductions (row sums, softmax) · T3 tiled (matmul, convolution) · T4 fused (attention, layernorm, GLU)
